content:
7 min read
Just now

--

What it actually takes to run agents in production inside a real enterprise — and why the demo tells you almost nothing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A proof of concept for an AI agent takes about a week. Someone on the team wires up LangChain or CrewAI, connects a couple of tools, writes a decent system prompt, and demos an agent that answers questions, calls an API, and completes a task end to end. Leadership watches, gets excited, and walks out believing that agents are simple and the roadmap just got shorter.
That demo is the most dangerous artifact in the building. Not because it’s fake — it works — but because it quietly skips every problem that makes production agentic systems genuinely hard. I’ve watched this movie a few times now, in enterprises with real monoliths, real customers, and real money on the line. This article is a field report of everything the POC hides.
If you’re an engineering leader, an architect, or a developer being asked to “add agents” to an existing enterprise system, this is for you.

Problem 1: Your business logic doesn’t live where your agents live
Picture a typical enterprise — call it Organization X. The core of the business runs on a Java Spring Boot monolith that’s been growing for a decade. Around it sits a constellation of microservices. And now there’s a separate Python team, genuinely skilled, comfortable with LangChain, LangSmith, CrewAI, Google’s ADK — pick your framework.
Here’s the catch: the agents they build can’t do anything useful, because the business logic that matters is trapped inside the monolith. The rules, the validations, the workflows that actually run the company — none of it is reachable from a Python process sitting outside. And rewriting the monolith is neither easy nor cost-efficient. Nobody is funding a two-year rewrite so that an agent can approve a refund.
So you look at your options, and both of them hurt.
Option one: expose the monolith through MCP servers. In theory, clean — wrap business capabilities as tools, let external agents call them. In practice, you end up with a sprawl of MCP servers, each needing its own authentication story, and every single agent step now pays a network round trip. An agent that reasons in ten steps makes ten trips across the wire. Latency compounds fast, and suddenly your “intelligent workflow” takes forty seconds to do what a service call did in two.
Option two: build the agents inside the monolith. Now you discover how thin the Java side of this ecosystem is. There are barely any enterprise-grade options. Say you manage to bring in Google ADK for Java — great, you have a framework. But you have no evaluation tooling. The eval ecosystem lives almost entirely in Python. So before you’ve shipped a single agent, you’re building your own eval suite from scratch, in-house, on your own dime.
This is the adoption tax, and nobody prices it into the POC. The POC was a greenfield Python script. Your company is not a greenfield Python script.

Problem 2: The failures that only show up in production
Suppose you get past the architecture problem. Now you meet the class of problems that never appear in a demo, because demos run once, on a happy path, with someone watching.
A production agent system doing crucial business work needs rigorous testing, evals, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, rollback mechanisms, guardrails, and groundedness checks. Each of those is a project, not a feature flag. And underneath all of them sits the problem everyone eventually hits: cost.
A badly designed agentic system burns tokens like nothing else you’ve operated. Millions of them, casually, because every reasoning step, every retry, every tool result re-fed into context is more tokens. Your first instinct is to switch to a cheaper model — and quality drops, details get lost, the agent starts making mistakes it didn’t make before. Your second instinct is to throw a frontier model at it — and now you’re paying premium rates for what may be overkill, while response latency climbs and users wait.
Then come the questions that sound paranoid until they happen to you:
What happens when the token budget runs out in the middle of a business transaction? Not before it starts — in the middle. Half the workflow is done, half isn’t. Do you round-robin across API keys? Keep a backup budget? Roll back the half-completed work? Most teams have no answer, because the POC never ran long enough to ask.
What happens when an agent gets stuck in a loop? It’ll happily consume your entire quota and slam into rate limits while retrying the same failing step. So you do the sensible thing and cap the number of LLM calls per agent. And then, a month later, a legitimate customer scenario shows up that genuinely needs more turns than your cap allows — and your own safety mechanism blocks a valid business case. Every guardrail you add creates a new edge case. That’s the nature of this work.
And then multi-provider support. Your platform is supposed to work with OpenAI, Gemini, and Anthropic interchangeably. Does it behave the same across all three? It doesn’t. Same prompts, different models, different tool-calling behavior, different failure modes. You’re now maintaining quality parity across a matrix you don’t control, and it shifts under you every time a provider ships a new version. Then an on-prem customer calls and says they want a specific provider we didn’t support.

Problem 3: The people you have aren’t the people this needs
This might be the least discussed problem and the most expensive one.
Agentic AI development is a paradigm shift from conventional software development. You cannot take a good Java, .NET, or Python developer and simply assign them an agent to build. They will build it — that’s the problem. They’ll build it the way they’ve built everything else, and it will be wrong in ways they can’t see.
I’ve seen the result. One system had a single monolithic agent handling hundreds of tasks, routed by a “request code” passed along with the user prompt, with every code and its behavior documented inside one enormous system prompt. A normal application developer built it, applying normal application instincts: one entry point, one dispatcher, one giant switch statement — except the switch statement was a prompt. What it should have been was a set of domain-specific, single-responsibility agents under an orchestrator. The developer wasn’t careless. They just didn’t know the discipline existed.
Testing is where it gets worse. I’ve seen multi-agent systems deployed to production with zero evals. Their entire quality story was unit tests. And since LLM outputs are non-deterministic, every time a prompt changed or the model’s responses shifted, the tests broke — so developers just edited the expected assertions until the tests passed again. Green pipeline, meaningless coverage. They had never heard of evals or LLM-as-judge, because nobody told them they were now building non-deterministic systems that need a completely different verification approach.
The learning curve is steep, and that’s before we even touch fine-tuning or LLMOps. Budget for it, or your first production agent will be a very confident liability.

Problem 4: The decisions are being made two levels above the knowledge
The skills gap doesn’t stop at developers.
Technical managers who aren’t deep enough in this space are making crucial architectural calls — often without an architect in the loop at all. And when architects are in the loop, some of them aren’t equipped for this domain either, because nothing in their prior twenty years prepared them for systems whose core component is probabilistic.
The pattern that follows is predictable: the POC creates confidence, the confidence creates commitments, and then the team spends months struggling to turn a demo into something robust, reliable, and secure — while the customer is already holding the promise.
And there’s one specific myth I keep running into among engineers and managers who haven’t built these systems: “If it’s performing poorly, increase the context window or upgrade to a bigger reasoning model.” This is a myth. A poorly designed agentic system cannot be rescued by a superior LLM. If your task decomposition is wrong, your tools are badly designed, your context is polluted, and you have no evals — a frontier model doesn’t fix any of that. It just produces more expensive, more articulate versions of the same failures.

And then you have to keep it alive
Everything above happens before day two. After that comes continuous monitoring of non-deterministic behavior, deployment strategies for systems where a prompt change is a production change, and scaling infrastructure whose per-request cost varies by an order of magnitude depending on how the agent decides to reason. None of your existing runbooks cover this.

The honest takeaway
None of this means “don’t build agents.” It means the gap between a POC and a production agentic system isn’t a matter of polish — it’s a different engineering discipline, with its own testing philosophy, its own cost model, its own failure modes, and its own skill requirements.
If I had to compress the lessons: build evals before you build features. Keep agents narrow and single-responsibility, with an orchestrator on top. Treat prompts as production code. Design for token exhaustion, loops, and provider differences on day one, not after the incident. And don’t let a one-week demo write checks that a one-year engineering effort has to cash.
The POC isn’t wrong. It’s just answering a much easier question than the one your business is actually asking.
